chain_of_thought: Crash shows abort -> __stack_chk_fail -> Java_com_intsig_nativelib_QREngine_decodeFile. This pattern strongly indicates stack canary corruption detected by compiler-inserted stack protector, i.e., some form of stack buffer overflow or out-of-bounds write in or below the JNI method implementation.

However, no detailed native stack frames beyond the JNI entry are given, and no allocator/sanitizer frames appear; only the canary check fails. I don’t see the internal function where the overwrite occurs, and I have no view of the actual C/C++ implementation or arguments passed to helpers (e.g., memcpy, sprintf, etc.).

The JNI method name suggests it processes a file path or image data for QR decoding. In a real app, that input is ultimately user-controlled (file chosen, image content, etc.). In the fuzzing harness, fuzz_one_input likely feeds arbitrary data to this JNI, which is plausible and usually mirrors untrusted-input handling in production.

Because __stack_chk_fail is only raised if the saved stack canary word was modified, the code must have written past a stack buffer, a local array, or corrupt return address area. This is clear evidence of unsafe memory handling (e.g., unchecked memcpy, sprintf, or custom parsing) within the JNI function or its callees.

The question is whether this is just a harness artifact (e.g., impossible null/size combinations) or a genuine vulnerability path. The JNI decodeFile signature presumably accepts a jstring/jbyteArray and maybe other primitive args. Fuzz harnesses usually call it the same way Java would, just with more varied and extreme values (e.g., very long strings, weird encodings). Unless app-level Java code clamps all inputs before reaching JNI (which I cannot verify here), the native code should withstand arbitrary length/contents without corrupting its stack. That it doesn’t implies a bug that would persist under realistic large or malformed inputs.

No evidence is present of an intentional abort/guard (e.g., explicit abort() on error). The abort here is solely due to __stack_chk_fail, which is a protection reacting to already-occurred memory corruption. This is almost always considered at least a high-severity issue because, without the canary, this class of overflow is often exploitable for code execution.

Given the limited context, I cannot pinpoint the exact vulnerable helper function, argument flow, or which buffer overflows. I also cannot confirm whether any Java-side validation exists. But the combination of (1) stack canary failure, (2) location in a user-input-facing JNI, and (3) typical QR/image parsing complexity makes it highly likely that this reflects a genuine memory safety vulnerability rather than a pure harness artifact.
Therefore, I classify it as a likely real vulnerability (stack-based buffer overflow / out-of-bounds write), with high but not absolute confidence due to lack of direct source or decompiled code.
reasons: __stack_chk_fail indicates stack canary corruption, implying a stack-based buffer overflow or similar memory corruption in native code.
Crash occurs inside Java_com_intsig_nativelib_QREngine_decodeFile, a JNI method that processes attacker-controllable file/image input in normal app use.
Abort is triggered by compiler-inserted protection, not by explicit app logic, suggesting unintended unsafe memory behavior rather than a benign guard or harness-only condition.

evidence.note: Top application-level native frame before abort; followed immediately by __stack_chk_fail, indicating stack canary corruption in or below this JNI function.
Standard glibc/compiler runtime abort used when a stack protector detects corruption of the stack canary value.
JNI method name and purpose (decodeFile) imply handling of user-supplied file/image data, providing a realistic untrusted input path to the vulnerable native code.

recommendations: Reverse-engineer and audit Java_com_intsig_nativelib_QREngine_decodeFile and its callees for stack-based buffers and unsafe operations (e.g., memcpy, sprintf, custom parsers) and add explicit bounds checks on all lengths and indexes derived from input.
Add robust parameter validation at both Java and JNI boundaries (e.g., maximum path length, image size, buffer sizes) before performing any stack-based operations.

Rebuild native code with full stack protection and, if possible, sanitizers (ASan/UBSan) in a dedicated test build to localize the exact overflow and confirm that fixes fully eliminate the corruption.
assumptions: The fuzz harness calls Java_com_intsig_nativelib_QREngine_decodeFile in a manner similar to how production Java code would, differing mainly in input variation and extremity.
decodeFile processes data ultimately derived from user-/attacker-controlled files or images, not only fixed internal resources.
No additional, unseen validation layers completely sanitize inputs before they reach the vulnerable native code.
limitations: No decompiled native code or internal stack frames beyond the JNI entry function are available, preventing precise identification of the overflowing buffer or operation.
No Java-side call graph or source is provided to confirm exact argument types, validation, or realistic input constraints.
The triage lacks detailed register/memory state at crash time, so the specific overwritten region and exploitability level cannot be conclusively determined.
